straight_beat.name: Lars Knutson
straight_beat.column:
The Twins lost again, but not because they didn’t try. They tried, they batted, they chased, and the box don’t lie—Toronto’s 10 runs were real, and Minnesota’s four were real too. The question isn’t whether they played hard. The question is why, when the team is supposed to be a playoff contender, they keep letting games slip away like water through a sieve.  

The pitching staff, for all its early-season promise, continues to frustrate. Tonight, it was the starters again—Kenta Maeda, who’s pitched well enough to make you forget he’s 34, walked four and allowed five runs in six innings. The bullpen, which should be a strength, became a liability. Jhustian Hererra, who’s been a rock in the seventh, allowed three runs in a row to start the eighth, and by the time the game was over, the Twins were already out of the race for the second wild

optimist.name: Marlene Holmqvist
optimist.column: The Twins lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour the day. Oh, ya sure, this is the year. I’ve been saying it since the streak broke in 2023, and I’ll say it again: this team’s got a heartbeat in the right place. A 7-7 record? That’s not a dead end—it’s a detour. The AL Central’s a crowded highway, but Minnesota’s got

pessimist.name: Gunnar 'Gunny' Bjornstad
pessimist.column:
The Twins lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your hat across the dugout and scream into the night. Four runs, ten runs. A game that should’ve been a coronation for the offense, and instead it’s another page in the playbook of mediocrity. Uff da, again.  

You know the script by now. The Pohlads run a bakery and the bread’s always stale. Every October they’re gone in three games and every April they’re telling me it’s different. It isn’t. This isn’t a new story. It’s the same old tale, just with different players and a slightly newer scoreboard. The Twins are a team that
